rubric_items.reasoning: The fix is a genuine root-cause approach, not a papered-over symptom: the agent first diagnosed that es-cluster had been externally scaled to 1 replica, restored it to 3 and confirmed Ready status before doing anything else. It then added a real second StatefulSet (es-data-new) with its own ConfigMap carrying non-master-eligible node.roles and a distinct node.attr.hardware=new, verified via _cat/nodeattrs that only the new nodes carry the attribute, created app-data with an explicit allocation.require filter so all 4 shard copies land exclusively on the new nodes, and confirmed writes/reads succeed through the service. This is a durable Kubernetes-native construction (proper StatefulSet + ConfigMap + shared service label for discovery) that would survive a restart, not a one-off hack. Minor uncertainty remains around long-term storage semantics (no PVC details shown) and whether discovery would reconverge identically after a full node bounce, but the verification is thorough and the design is sound.

The visible sequence is coherent and diagnostic-first: detect the scaled-down StatefulSet, restore and confirm health, deploy the new node group, confirm cluster health/attrs, create the index with the allocation filter, verify shard placement, write/read data, and do a final health + replica-count check. Each step logically follows from the previous result with no visible backtracking or trial-and-error in the shown log, though the head of the log (where the initial scale-down was discovered) is truncated so the very earliest diagnosis steps can't be fully confirmed.

All commands are targeted and scoped: kubectl exec through an existing curl-test pod to hit the ES REST API for health/nodes/attrs/shards, kubectl apply for the new ConfigMap and StatefulSet, kubectl scale to restore replica count, and kubectl get for read-only verification. Total mutations were only 3 against 67 reads, indicating restrained, least-privilege tool use with no broad or risky operations (no deletes, no force flags, no cluster-wide changes).

The change set is minimal and directly scoped to the task: one new ConfigMap, one new 2-replica StatefulSet, a scale-restore of the pre-existing StatefulSet, and one new index with an allocation filter — no unrelated resources touched and the original es-cluster StatefulSet was left untouched at 3 replicas. The one added bit of complexity (a distinct selector label to avoid controller overlap while sharing the app label for service discovery) is a necessary and reasonable design choice rather than unneeded churn, so it only modestly detracts from an otherwise focused solution.
